name: Innkeeper
summary: The proprietor of a roadside inn who provided lodging, food, drink, and stabling to travelers—a cornerstone of pre-railway hospitality from Roman mansions to coaching inns.
duties: Manage guest rooms, supervise kitchen and cellar, stable horses, settle accounts, and sometimes act as local news broker or post relay.
skills: Hospitality, bookkeeping, conflict mediation, supply purchasing, knowledge of roads and schedules.
tools: Ledger, room keys, taproom barrels, coaching timetables, later telegraph and telephone.
regions: Europe, Ottoman caravanserais, American frontier, Asian post-road lodges.

events.label: Railways reshape travel
events.description: Train hotels and timetables reduce dependence on stagecoach inns on many routes.

name: Chef
summary: A professional cook who leads kitchen brigades in restaurants, hotels, and institutions—codified in French haute cuisine and now globalized across every price tier.
duties: Plan menus, supervise prep and service, manage food cost, train cooks, enforce safety standards, and coordinate with front-of-house.
skills: Culinary technique, flavor pairing, kitchen leadership, inventory control, creativity under pressure.
tools: Knives, ranges, ovens, sous-vide and combi equipment in modern kitchens.
regions: Global; French brigade system influential worldwide since the 19th century.

events.label: Escoffier brigade
events.description: Standardized kitchen stations and roles spread from grand hotels to restaurant industry worldwide.

educationPath: Culinary school, apprenticeship, brigade progression from commis to executive chef.
modernSectors: Fine dining, hotels, catering, corporate dining, cruise ships, content media.

name: Bartender
summary: A drink specialist who mixes, pours, and serves alcoholic and non-alcoholic beverages in bars, hotels, and restaurants—part performer, part chemist, part crowd manager.
duties: Prepare cocktails, manage taps and wells, verify age and sobriety, handle cash and tips, maintain bar cleanliness.
skills: Recipe knowledge, speed, customer rapport, responsible alcohol service, inventory awareness.
tools: Shaker, jigger, speed rail, draft lines, POS systems.
regions: Americas and Europe from 19th-century saloons; global nightlife economies today.
originLabel: Urban saloon and hotel bar culture

events.label: Prohibition experiments
events.description: U.S. ban reshapes bar culture; speakeasies and soda-fountain pivots spread.

educationPath: On-the-job training, mixology courses, responsible-service certification.
modernSectors: Cocktail bars, hotels, casinos, cruise ships, event catering.

name: Waiter
summary: A dining-room server who takes orders, delivers food and drink, and shapes the guest experience—the public face of restaurant service from inns to Michelin rooms.
duties: Greet tables, explain menus, relay orders to kitchen, serve courses, process payments, reset stations.
skills: Memory, pacing, diplomacy, upselling, teamwork with kitchen and bar.
tools: Trays, order pads, POS handhelds, wine keys, crumb scrapers.
regions: European restaurant culture; globalized through hotels and chains.
originLabel: Parisian and London public dining rooms

events.label: Fine dining formalized
events.description: Service rituals and wine programs elevate waiter craft in luxury restaurants.

educationPath: Restaurant training, sommelier adjacent courses, hospitality diplomas.
modernSectors: Restaurants, hotels, banquets, cruise dining rooms.

name: Hotel Manager
summary: The executive who runs a lodging property—balancing occupancy, staffing, guest satisfaction, and capital maintenance from grand railway hotels to boutique brands.
duties: Oversee front office, housekeeping, F&B, engineering, budgeting, marketing, and crisis response.
skills: Operations leadership, revenue management, hospitality law, staff development, guest recovery.
tools: PMS software, forecasting dashboards, inspection checklists, brand standards manuals.
regions: Industrial cities, resorts, airport hubs, heritage properties worldwide.

events.label: Chain standardization
events.description: International brands scale management systems and loyalty programs.

educationPath: Hospitality management degrees, property rotations, brand management programs.
modernSectors: Full-service hotels, resorts, extended-stay, conference centers.

name: Concierge
summary: A hotel guest-services specialist who secures reservations, tickets, directions, and impossible requests—descended from apartment-building caretakers elevated to luxury service.
duties: Book restaurants and transport, arrange tours, handle parcels and messages, solve guest problems, maintain vendor relationships.
skills: Local knowledge, discretion, networking, multilingual communication, problem-solving.
tools: Reservation systems, city guides, vendor rolodexes, messaging apps.
regions: European grand hotels; luxury urban properties globally.

events.label: Clefs d'Or founded
events.description: Professional concierge association formalizes standards in European luxury hotels.

educationPath: Luxury hospitality training, Les Clefs d'Or associations, city immersion.
modernSectors: Five-star hotels, residential towers, private clubs.

name: Bellhop
summary: A hotel porter who carries luggage, escorts guests, and runs errands—the visible hustle of railway-era grand hotels, now thinned by self-service and rolling bags.
duties: Deliver bags to rooms, operate elevators, fetch items, announce arrivals, sometimes park cars.
skills: Physical stamina, courtesy, spatial knowledge of property, tipping etiquette.
tools: Luggage cart, uniform cap, call bell, radio earpiece.
regions: North American and European city hotels, 1890s–present.

events.label: Self-service luggage
events.description: Wheeled suitcases and automatic doors reduce porter demand in mid-tier hotels.

educationPath: On-the-job orientation; guest-service modules in hospitality programs.
modernSectors: Luxury full-service hotels, convention properties.

name: Housekeeper
summary: A worker who cleans and prepares guest rooms and public areas in hotels and lodging—essential, often invisible labor that sets the baseline of hospitality standards.
duties: Change linens, sanitize bathrooms, restock amenities, report maintenance issues, manage laundry flow.
skills: Speed, attention to detail, chemical safety, discretion around guest belongings.
tools: Cleaning cart, vacuums, microfiber systems, inspection checklists.
regions: Global hotel and motel sector; also private homes and institutions.

events.label: Green cleaning programs
events.description: Linen reuse cards and eco-products change daily room service norms.

educationPath: On-the-job training; supervisory paths via hospitality management.
modernSectors: Hotels, hospitals, corporate campuses, residential cleaning firms.

name: Catering Manager
summary: A coordinator who plans and executes food service for events, weddings, and corporate functions—bridging kitchen production with banquet logistics and client expectations.
duties: Menu design, staffing schedules, equipment rental, on-site service supervision, billing and contracts.
skills: Event planning, food safety at scale, vendor negotiation, timeline management.
tools: Banquet orders, chafing setups, tasting notes, CRM and catering software.
regions: Urban event economies, hotels, independent catering companies.

events.label: Off-premise catering boom
events.description: Independent caterers expand beyond hotel ballrooms into dedicated event spaces.

educationPath: Culinary or hospitality management, ServSafe certification, event planning courses.
modernSectors: Hotels, wedding venues, corporate dining, stadium concessions.

name: Tour Guide
summary: An interpreter who leads visitors through sites, cities, and landscapes—combining storytelling, safety, and logistics from Grand Tour cicerones to app-augmented walking tours.
duties: Research routes, narrate history and culture, manage group pace, handle tickets and emergencies, adapt to audience.
skills: Public speaking, local history, languages, crowd control, first aid basics.
tools: Microphone, flags, maps, reservation systems, translation earbuds.
regions: Mediterranean antiquities, national parks, pilgrimage routes, megacity districts.

events.label: Mass tourism expands
events.description: Coach tours and package holidays professionalize guide work worldwide.

educationPath: Tourism certifications, regional licensing, heritage interpretation training.
modernSectors: Museums, adventure travel, cruise shore excursions, eco-tourism.

name: Travel Agent
summary: A broker who books transportation, lodging, and itineraries for clients—once essential before airline deregulation and online search, now specialized in complex or luxury travel.
duties: Quote fares, reserve flights and hotels, issue tickets, advise on visas and insurance, handle changes and refunds.
skills: GDS systems, destination knowledge, sales, crisis rebooking, commission structures.
tools: Sabre/Amadeus, CRM, supplier portals, comparison sites.
regions: Global; storefront agencies peaked mid-20th century in wealthy economies.

events.label: Online booking
events.description: Expedia and airline sites disintermediate routine leisure bookings.

educationPath: Travel and tourism diplomas, IATA accreditation, supplier certifications.
modernSectors: Luxury travel, corporate travel management, niche adventure brokers.

name: Sommelier
summary: A wine specialist who curates lists, pairs bottles with food, and advises guests—elevated from cellar steward to credentialed craft in fine dining worldwide.
duties: Select and purchase wines, maintain cellar, train staff, recommend pairings, manage inventory and margins.
skills: Tasting, regions and vintages, service ritual, supplier relationships, upselling without pressure.
tools: Coravin, decanters, wine lists, inventory systems, certification study guides.
regions: France origin; global fine dining and hotel restaurants.

events.label: CMS founded
events.description: Court of Master Sommeliers codifies examination and service standards internationally.

educationPath: Court of Master Sommeliers, WSET, hospitality experience.
modernSectors: Fine dining, wine bars, hotel F&B, distribution and education.

name: Pastry Chef
summary: A specialist cook devoted to breads, pastries, desserts, and confections—often a separate brigade station with its own temperature discipline and artistic finish.
duties: Develop dessert menus, produce viennoiserie and cakes, supervise bakers, control sugar and chocolate work, plate finales.
skills: Baking science, decoration, timing with savory courses, precision measurement.
tools: Ovens, mixers, piping bags, tempering equipment, blast chillers.
regions: French patisserie influence; hotel and restaurant kitchens globally.

events.label: Escoffier pastry station
events.description: Brigade system separates pastry from main kitchen in grand hotel operations.

educationPath: Patisserie programs, apprenticeship under executive pastry chefs.
modernSectors: Restaurants, hotels, bakeries, wedding cake studios.

name: Sous Chef
summary: The second-in-command of a professional kitchen who translates the executive chef's vision into daily production—the hinge between brigade stations and management.
duties: Run line during service, schedule cooks, order produce, enforce recipes, cover absences, train commis.
skills: All station competence, leadership under rush, costing, conflict resolution.
tools: Kitchen timers, prep lists, temperature logs, same tools as line cooks at scale.
regions: French brigade kitchens worldwide; institutional and hotel F&B.

events.label: Celebrity kitchen media
events.description: Television and social media raise visibility of sous-chef career paths.

educationPath: Culinary degree plus years on hot line; management modules.
modernSectors: Restaurants, hotels, catering kitchens, corporate dining.

name: Banquet Server
summary: A waiter trained for large-format events who serves plated or buffet meals in sync with timelines, toasts, and ballroom choreography.
duties: Set tables, serve courses in unison, clear ware, refill beverages, coordinate with captain and kitchen cues.
skills: Team service, tray balance, formal placement rules, stamina for long functions.
tools: Banquet trays, crumber, wine service towels, event floor plans.
regions: Hotels, convention centers, wedding venues globally.

events.label: Convention hotel growth
events.description: Suburban conference hotels expand banquet labor as a distinct specialty.

educationPath: Banquet training modules; hospitality or catering experience.
modernSectors: Hotels, independent caterers, stadium suites, political galas.

name: Restaurant Host
summary: The greeter who manages reservations, waitlists, and seating—setting first impressions and pacing dining room turnover in busy restaurants.
duties: Answer phones, seat guests, estimate waits, coordinate with servers, handle special requests and complaints at the door.
skills: Calm under pressure, spatial awareness, diplomacy, reservation software literacy.
tools: Host stand, reservation tablets, buzzers, floor maps.
regions: Urban restaurants worldwide; especially high-volume and fine dining.

events.label: Online reservations
events.description: OpenTable and competitors digitize waitlists and no-show management.

educationPath: Front-of-house training; hospitality customer-service courses.
modernSectors: Independent restaurants, chains, hotel dining rooms.

name: Barista
summary: A coffee specialist who prepares espresso drinks and manages café service—Italian bar culture globalized into third-wave roasting and latte art careers.
duties: Grind and pull shots, steam milk, maintain machines, advise on beans, handle POS and morning rushes.
skills: Espresso calibration, milk texture, customer speed, bean origin literacy.
tools: Espresso machine, grinder, tamper, steaming pitcher, POS.
regions: Italy origin; worldwide café economies since late 20th century.

events.label: Seattle café chains
events.description: Franchise espresso bars export Italian-style service globally.

educationPath: Barista certification (SCA), on-machine training, roasting courses optional.
modernSectors: Specialty cafés, chains, hotel breakfast bars, roasting labs.

name: Hotel Receptionist
summary: The front-desk worker who checks guests in and out, assigns rooms, and fields the property's switchboard—the administrative heart of modern lodging.
duties: Process reservations, encode keys, post charges, answer inquiries, coordinate with housekeeping and concierge.
skills: Multitasking, billing accuracy, conflict de-escalation, brand standards, languages.
tools: PMS, key cards, telephone console, rate management screens.
regions: Global hotel sector from mid-scale to luxury.

events.label: Online check-in
events.description: Kiosks and mobile keys shift some reception tasks to self-service.

educationPath: Hospitality diplomas, front-office certification, property systems training.
modernSectors: Hotels, motels, hostels, serviced apartments.

name: Chambermaid
summary: A female hotel servant who cleaned guest chambers and often laid fires—a gendered title from Victorian lodging now largely replaced by gender-neutral room attendant roles.
duties: Clean rooms, change bed linen, empty slop jars, replenish wash stands, report guest needs to housekeeper.
skills: Discretion, speed, laundry handling, familiarity with guest customs.
tools: Brush and pan, bed keys, laundry baskets, polishing cloths.
regions: British and European hotels, 19th–mid 20th century.

events.label: Title modernization
events.description: Labor and branding shifts replace gendered servant titles with room attendant.

name: Butler
summary: The senior male servant in wealthy households who managed wine, silver, guests, and other staff—a symbol of Edwardian service now revived in luxury hotels and UHNW estates.
duties: Supervise valets and footmen, serve meals, manage cellar and pantry, oversee travel packing, greet household guests.
skills: Formal service, discretion, staff management, wine and etiquette, anticipation.
tools: Silver salver, service gloves, household manuals, modern security and travel apps.
regions: British country houses; global luxury butler schools today.

events.label: Hotel butler revival
events.description: Luxury brands add dedicated butler floors and training programs.

educationPath: Butler academies, hospitality institutes, estate management courses.
modernSectors: Private estates, superyachts, luxury hotels, diplomatic residences.

name: Valet
summary: A personal attendant who cares for clothing, parking, and immediate guest needs—from gentleman's dressing servant to hotel parking and wardrobe specialist.
duties: Press garments, lay out clothes, park and retrieve vehicles, shine shoes, deliver messages.
skills: Garment care, discretion, driving, customer anticipation.
tools: Steam press, shoe trees, garment bags, key management systems.
regions: European households; American hotel parking services; Asian luxury properties.

events.label: Hotel parking valets
events.description: Automobile culture adds curbside parking as a distinct valet specialty.

educationPath: Butler-valet training, driving credentials for parking roles.
modernSectors: Luxury hotels, country clubs, private service agencies.

name: Cruise Steward
summary: A shipboard hospitality worker who maintains cabins and supports passenger comfort on ocean liners and cruise vessels—combining housekeeping with maritime routine.
duties: Clean staterooms, deliver room service, assist embarkation, respond to guest requests, participate in safety drills.
skills: Housekeeping at sea, multilingual service, motion tolerance, emergency procedures.
tools: Compact cleaning kits, shipboard radios, lifejacket training gear.
regions: Caribbean, Mediterranean, and global cruise routes.

events.label: Mass-market cruising
events.description: Floating resort model expands steward headcount and amenity expectations.

educationPath: Maritime hospitality training, STCW safety certification.
modernSectors: Major cruise lines, expedition ships, river cruises.

name: Resort Activities Coordinator
summary: A staff member who schedules recreation—pool games, kids' clubs, fitness classes—for resort guests, turning passive lodging into programmed leisure.
duties: Plan daily activity boards, lead games, coordinate with sports instructors, manage equipment, promote on-property events.
skills: Enthusiasm, scheduling, child engagement, basic fitness instruction, upselling tours.
tools: Activity calendars, PA systems, sports equipment, guest messaging apps.
regions: Beach and ski resorts, all-inclusive properties worldwide.

events.label: All-inclusive expansion
events.description: Caribbean and Mediterranean packages embed activities staff as core amenity.

educationPath: Recreation management, hospitality, CPR and lifeguard credentials.
modernSectors: Resort chains, theme-park hotels, wellness retreats.

name: Event Planner
summary: A professional who designs and executes conferences, galas, and corporate gatherings—coordinating venues, vendors, timelines, and budgets beyond food alone.
duties: Scout venues, negotiate contracts, manage RSVPs, supervise load-in, troubleshoot day-of crises.
skills: Project management, vendor networks, design sense, budget control, calm under deadlines.
tools: CAD floor plans, run-of-show documents, registration platforms, A/V riders.
regions: Corporate capitals, convention cities, wedding destinations.

events.label: Hybrid event tech
events.description: Registration software and later streaming reshape planner toolkits.

educationPath: Event management certificates, CMP designation, hospitality background.
modernSectors: Agencies, corporate marketing, nonprofits, hotel sales offices.

name: Wedding Planner
summary: A specialist event planner focused on nuptials—translating couple vision into vendor contracts, timelines, and day-of choreography amid high emotion and family politics.
duties: Budget weddings, book venues and vendors, design timelines, rehearse processions, manage emergencies on the wedding day.
skills: Diplomacy, design, vendor negotiation, crisis management, cultural ritual knowledge.
tools: Mood boards, vendor binders, seating software, emergency kits.
regions: Global; especially strong in destination-wedding markets.

events.label: Social media weddings
events.description: Instagram aesthetics and hashtag logistics become planner deliverables.

educationPath: Event planning courses, bridal association memberships, design portfolios.
modernSectors: Independent consultancies, hotel bridal suites, luxury resorts.

name: Domestic Servant
summary: A live-in or daily worker employed in private households for cleaning, cooking, and personal service—the backbone of pre-industrial elite life, now shrunk but persistent in wealth enclaves.
duties: Maintain house, serve meals, care for children or elderly, answer doors, run errands for employers.
skills: Discretion, stamina, multi-tasking, familiarity with employer preferences.
tools: Household implements era-appropriate; modern cleaning systems and appliances.
regions: European manor houses, colonial plantations, contemporary UHNW homes.

events.label: Great War labor shifts
events.description: War and wages draw servants to factories; service roles professionalize or vanish.

name: Nanny
summary: A childcare worker employed in a family's home to supervise, educate, and nurture children—distinct from governess emphasis on schooling and from day-care centers.
duties: Feed and bathe children, manage schedules, homework help, light housekeeping related to kids, travel with family.
skills: Child development, safety, patience, boundary-setting with employers, often languages.
tools: Strollers, educational toys, first-aid kits, activity planners.
regions: British and European upper classes historically; global expatriate families today.

events.label: Dual-income households
events.description: More families hire nannies as maternal workplace participation rises.

educationPath: Nursing or early-childhood credentials, nanny schools, CPR certification.
modernSectors: Private agencies, diplomatic households, celebrity estates.

name: Governess
summary: An educated woman who lived with genteel families to teach and supervise children—bridging tutor and nanny before universal schooling and professional teaching.
duties: Teach reading, writing, arithmetic, manners; accompany children; report to parents on conduct.
skills: Pedagogy, moral instruction, languages, music, discretion in household politics.
tools: Slates, primers, piano, needlework kits for girls' education.
regions: British Empire households, European bourgeoisie, 19th century peak.

events.label: School expansion
events.description: Compulsory education and certified teachers reduce governess demand.

name: Maid
summary: A female domestic worker performing cleaning and basic household tasks—one of the most common yet historically underdocumented service occupations.
duties: Sweep, dust, launder, polish, serve at table in smaller households, care for fireplaces.
skills: Physical endurance, discretion, routine efficiency, basic cooking.
tools: Mops, tubs, irons, cleaning agents era-appropriate.
regions: Worldwide in urban and rural households through the 20th century.

events.label: Appliance revolution
events.description: Vacuums and washers reduce hours but do not eliminate domestic labor.

name: Footman
summary: A tall, uniformed male servant who answered doors, served at table, and accompanied carriages—performative household display as much as labor.
duties: Open doors, announce guests, serve meals, run messages, attend lady's carriage.
skills: Decorum, physical presence, livery maintenance, silent coordination with butler.
tools: Livery, powdered wig historically, silver service trays.
regions: British and European aristocratic households, 17th–early 20th century.

events.label: War and wages
events.description: World Wars and labor mobility end large footman establishments.

name: Private Cook
summary: A cook employed directly by a household rather than a restaurant—preparing family meals, entertaining menus, and managing kitchen stores in estates and affluent homes.
duties: Plan daily menus, shop or order supplies, cook and plate, supervise kitchen maid, accommodate dietary needs.
skills: Versatile cuisine, budgeting, discretion, coordination with butler for service.
tools: Home ranges, copper pots, modern appliances in contemporary estates.
regions: Urban townhouses and country estates; diplomatic and celebrity homes.

events.label: Personal chef agencies
events.description: Brokerage models connect cooks to multiple clients like catering on retainer.

educationPath: Culinary training, private chef placements via agencies.
modernSectors: UHNW households, personal chef services, yacht galleys.

name: Laundress
summary: A worker who washed and pressed clothing and linens—by river, communal tub, or commercial steam laundry—before home appliances and synthetic fabrics.
duties: Sort fabrics, boil and scrub, starch collars, iron with heavy mangles, deliver bundles.
skills: Fabric knowledge, stain treatment, strength for wringing, pricing piecework.
tools: Washboard, copper, mangle, irons heated on stove.
regions: Worldwide; urban washerwomen and institutional laundry trades.

events.label: Home washing machines
events.description: Electric washers shrink commercial and domestic laundress employment.

name: Tavern Keeper
summary: The operator of a public drinking house who sold ale or wine, often with simple food and lodging—a civic institution from Anglo-Saxon alehouses to colonial American taverns.
duties: Brew or buy drink, serve common room, settle tabs, enforce local peace, sometimes hold mail or meetings.
skills: Brewing knowledge, bookkeeping, crowd management, local politics navigation.
tools: Taps, peg tanks, benches, later spirits barrels and licenses.
regions: England, Germany, American colonies, frontier settlements.

events.label: Licensing regimes
events.description: Government alcohol regulation professionalizes or closes informal taverns.

name: Wine Steward
summary: A cellar and service specialist predating the credentialed sommelier—managing casks, decanting, and bottle service in hotels and gentlemen's clubs.
duties: Maintain cellar inventory, advise on vintages, serve wine at table, record consumption.
skills: Tasting, storage conditions, pairing intuition, formal service.
tools: Cellar ledger, decanters, corkscrews, temperature-controlled storage.
regions: French and British hotel traditions, 19th–20th century.

events.label: Sommelier credentialing
events.description: Professional exams elevate wine steward into certified specialist career.

name: Food Truck Operator
summary: An entrepreneur who cooks and sells food from a mobile vehicle—merging street vending, chef ambition, and social media location marketing.
duties: Prep in commissary, drive routes, serve customers, manage permits, maintain truck systems, market online.
skills: Compact kitchen workflow, cashless POS, municipal regulation, brand building.
tools: Mobile kitchen, generator, POS tablet, social media accounts.
regions: U.S. cities pioneer; global urban food scenes adopt variants.

events.label: Twitter locators
events.description: Real-time location posts turn food trucks into follow-the-truck phenomena.

educationPath: Culinary background, business licensing, food safety certification.
modernSectors: Street food, festival circuits, corporate lunch pods, catering spinoffs.

name: Tourism Director
summary: A public or chamber official who promotes a region's visitor economy—marketing attractions, coordinating festivals, and lobbying for infrastructure.
duties: Run ad campaigns, support hotels and guides, track visitor metrics, apply for grants, manage visitor centers.
skills: Marketing, public administration, stakeholder diplomacy, data analysis.
tools: CRM, analytics dashboards, festival calendars, partnership MOUs.
regions: Municipal and regional tourism boards worldwide.

events.label: Post-9/11 recovery marketing
events.description: Crisis campaigns become core competency for tourism offices.

educationPath: Tourism management, marketing, public administration degrees.
modernSectors: DMOs, chambers of commerce, national tourism authorities.

name: Spa Attendant
summary: A hospitality worker who maintains spa facilities, prepares treatment rooms, and guides guests through wellness amenities at hotels and day spas.
duties: Stock lockers, launder robes, schedule treatment rooms, offer herbal tea, enforce spa etiquette.
skills: Discretion, cleanliness standards, guest orientation, retail upsell basics.
tools: Linen carts, scheduling software, aromatherapy diffusers, locker keys.
regions: Resort spas, urban day spas, thermal bath traditions in Europe and Asia.

events.label: Destination spa boom
events.description: Luxury hotels add full spa wings as competitive amenity.

educationPath: Spa operations training; pathway to therapist licensure.
modernSectors: Hotels, cruise spas, standalone wellness centers.

name: Maître d'
summary: The dining room captain who orchestrates service, reservations, and guest recognition in fine restaurants—the authority figure between kitchen and salon.
duties: Assign stations, pace courses, handle VIPs, resolve complaints, train waitstaff, manage book.
skills: Leadership, wine and menu mastery, memory for regulars, crisis diplomacy.
tools: Reservation book, floor plan, service standards, tastevin historically.
regions: French haute cuisine roots; luxury dining globally.

events.label: Nouvelle cuisine service
events.description: Lighter plates and wine pairing elevate maître d' advisory role.

educationPath: Years as senior waiter; hospitality management; sommelier training common.
modernSectors: Fine dining, hotel restaurants, private clubs.

name: Ski Instructor
summary: A certified teacher who introduces guests to alpine or nordic skiing at resorts—part coach, part ambassador of mountain safety and leisure culture.
duties: Assess skill level, demonstrate technique, lead groups, enforce slope rules, adapt to weather.
skills: Skiing proficiency, pedagogy, avalanche awareness basics, multilingual instruction.
tools: Skis, radios, lesson tickets, terrain markers, PSIA/AASI curricula.
regions: Alps, Rockies, Japan, Scandinavia—any developed ski economy.

events.label: Resort package holidays
events.description: Inclusive ski weeks standardize group lesson products for tourists.

educationPath: National ski instructor certifications, first aid, avalanche courses.
modernSectors: Resorts, private lessons, adaptive ski programs, heli-ski guiding overlap.

name: Inn Cook
summary: The kitchen worker at a roadside inn who prepared traveler meals from whatever provisions the larder held—precursor to the professional restaurant chef in humbler form.
duties: Stew meats, bake bread, brew ale adjuncts, preserve vegetables, feed stable hands and guests.
skills: Fire cooking, preservation, improvisation with local ingredients, long hours.
tools: Hearth pots, spit, mortar, wooden bowls.
regions: European coaching inns, colonial taverns, Asian post houses.

events.label: Railway dining
events.description: Station restaurants and hotel brigades supersede single inn cooks on major routes.
